text: We would like to thank the Society for the honor of consideration of our work. Today we are presenting a robot-assisted transabdominal preperitoneal or TAP ventral hernia repair for a patient with a multi-recurrent incisional hernia with incarcerated bladder. We have no disclosures relevant to this presentation.

Our patient is a sixty-two-year-old male with history of an open appendectomy in nineteen eighty-two, complicated by a multi-recurrent incisional hernia. He presented to our hernia center with a complaint of a one-year history of a right lower quadrant bulge associated with only mild pain at baseline, but with increasing hesitancy and pain associated with voiding. Past medical history includes type two diabetes, GERD, BPH, and hyperlipidemia. He is a never smoker with a BMI of thirty-one point three. Surgical history included the aforementioned open appendectomy, as well as a primary umbilical hernia repair. Previous incisional hernia repairs included three separate laparoscopic repairs, all with intraperitoneal mesh placement. Upon evaluation in clinic, the patient was noted to have a partially reducible right lower quadrant incisional hernia recurrent at the site of previous open appendectomy. Preoperative CT confirmed the recurrent hernia with evidence of incarcerated bladder and also noted significant intraperitoneal mesh.
The patient was taken to the operating room with plan to complete a robotic tap right-sided TAR and mesh excision.

After induction, bilateral TAP blocks are completed and a Foley catheter is placed. Abdominal access is achieved using a Veress needle at the left costal margin, followed by insufflation to 15 mmHg. An 8 mm robotic port replaces the Veress needle, and the abdomen is inspected with a 30-degree robotic laparoscope. Additional 8 mm ports are placed under direct visualization in the left mid-abdomen and left lower quadrant. The da Vinci robot is docked on the patient's left flank.

The surgeon dissects adhesions between the small bowel and abdominal wall, including those attached to previously placed mesh, using sharp, blunt, and electrocautery techniques. The right-sided retrorectus dissection begins with division of the fused linea alba adjacent to the hernia using cautery, revealing the rectus muscle. The posterior rectus sheath is released from the rectus muscle with scissors and cautery, extending the plane 5 cm above the defect. Lateral dissection exposes neurovascular bundles at the rectus muscle's lateral extent, completing the first myofascial flap. Attention shifts to the right-sided transversus abdominis release (TAR), where the transversus abdominis muscle is divided medial to the neurovascular bundles using electrocautery and sharp dissection, extending from the xiphoid to the arcuate line. This exposes the transversalis fascia and peritoneum. The transversus abdominis muscle is mobilized anteriorly, creating a wide peritoneal flap laterally into the retroperitoneum. An assistant port is placed in the right mid-abdomen, and a Raytech surgical sponge is introduced into the retromuscular plane for traction during TAR development.

The surgeon meticulously dissects and reduces the hernia sac and contents, including the incarcerated bladder, using blunt and sharp dissection along with electrosurgical energy. The previous mesh and tacks are carefully excised, freeing them from dense adhesions to the bladder and preperitoneal adipose tissue. The dissection extends inferiorly beyond the hernia defect to expose the pubic symphysis and the myopectenial orifice, and laterally to reveal the psoas muscle, completing the second myofascial flap and allowing for generous mesh overlap and placement in a virgin plane.

The surgeon excises additional components of previously placed mesh, carefully freeing them from attachments to the previously incarcerated bladder. The excised mesh is cut in a pinwheel fashion to facilitate removal through the assistant port.

The surgeon encloses the anterior rectus sheath, including all defects, using a running suture with a number one Stratafix. The hernia sac is incorporated into the closure to minimize dead space and reduce seroma formation. The abdomen is desufflated to ten millimeters mercury insufflation pressure. Methylene blue is infused into the previously incarcerated bladder, achieving full distention, and the abdomen is inspected for signs of injury indicated by methylene blue extravasation.

The surgeon cuts the progrit mesh to size and introduces it into the abdomen through the assistant trocar in the right upper quadrant. The mesh is carefully unfolded and positioned behind the defect, ensuring excellent overlap and close approximation with the overlying tissue. The abdomen remains desufflated to ten millimeters mercury during this process. Suture for closing the posterior sheath is brought into the abdomen via the assistant port, which is subsequently removed, with the port site covered by the inserted mesh.

The surgeon ensures hemostasis after mesh placement and re-approximates the posterior sheath to the anterior abdominal wall using a running III-O Stratific suture. The remaining eight-millimeter ports are removed under direct visualization, and the abdomen is fully desufflated. The skin of all port sites is closed with running subcuticular sutures using Monocryl.
At four-week follow-up, he expressed no current concern for hernia recurrence, was voiding normally, and steadily returning to previous activity consistent with an expected post-operative course.
